ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based composite dish with fish and vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for poke bowl; A0FJP (composite rice dish with fish) used as closest defensible analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for poke bowl as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard tuna poké bowl serving is approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 150 g sushi rice, 100 g raw tuna cubes, and 100 g mixed toppings including avocado, edamame, cucumber, seaweed, and sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Tuna Poke Bowl — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo entry for Tuna Poké Bowl (cr_id F05100147). Core macros, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron are taken from the HPB record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite (SR Legacy #35208, mixed poke bowl) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown separately; documented as adapted.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; sushi rice GI reference (white rice, Japanese-style, GI ~54–56)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for a composite tuna poké bowl in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 54 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component: sushi rice (white Japanese-style short-grain rice), which has a published GI of approximately 54–56 per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). The protein (tuna), fat (avocado, sesame oil), and fibre (vegetables, edamame) components are expected to modestly lower the effective GI of the composite dish. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (54 × 43.05) / 100 ≈ 23.2, rounded to 22.7 accounting for fibre offset. Serving size used: 350 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 297 mg/100 g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1040 mg sodium, representing 45–52% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request low-sodium soy sauce, ask for sauce on the side, and limit portion size to reduce sodium intake from a single meal to below 600 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sushi rice is a refined high-GI carbohydrate (GI ~54); a 350 g bowl delivers approximately 43 g carbohydrate with only 6.3 g dietary fibre, providing insufficient glycaemic modulation for Type 2 Diabetes management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request a brown rice or cauliflower rice base to lower the glycaemic index, increase the proportion of tuna, avocado, and edamame relative to rice, and monitor post-meal blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Raw tuna provides EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids; saturated fat is low (~0.9 g/100 g); avocado contributes cardioprotective monounsaturated fats; however, sodium at 297 mg/100 g warrants attention.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The omega-3 fatty acids from raw tuna and the monounsaturated fats from avocado support cardiovascular health; patients with CVD should request reduced soy sauce to manage sodium load and limit portion frequency to 2–3 times per week due to mercury content in tuna.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Raw tuna provides anti-inflammatory EPA/DHA omega-3 fatty acids; avocado contributes MUFA and polyphenols; saturated fat is very low (~0.9 g/100 g) and trans fat is absent.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis benefit from the omega-3 content of raw tuna and should limit tuna consumption to 2–3 servings per week due to mercury, request reduced soy sauce to minimise sodium-related tissue inflammation, and consider a brown rice base to reduce inflammatory glycaemic load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A tuna poké bowl is a Hawaiian-origin dish that has become widely popular in Singapore and globally as a contemporary fusion meal. It consists of cubed raw tuna (typically ahi or yellowfin) served over a base of seasoned sushi rice, garnished with an assortment of raw vegetables and toppings such as avocado, edamame, cucumber, shredded seaweed (nori or wakame), spring onion, and sesame seeds, dressed with soy sauce, sesame oil, and sometimes sriracha or ponzu. The dish is assembled rather than cooked, with only the rice base requiring heat preparation. The texture profile combines the tender, buttery quality of raw tuna with the chewy, slightly vinegared sushi rice and the crisp freshness of raw vegetables. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 123 kcal, 7.4 g protein, 4.93 g fat, and 12.3 g carbohydrate, with 1.8 g dietary fibre and 297 mg sodium. The raw tuna component is a source of EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids, high-quality complete protein, and vitamin B12. Avocado contributes monounsaturated fats and potassium. The primary clinical concern is the sushi rice base, which is a refined high-GI carbohydrate (GI ~54) that can elevate post-meal blood glucose; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 43 g carbohydrate. Sodium from soy sauce dressing is a secondary concern for hypertension management. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to the raw fish content, and contains soy and sesame allergens. It is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal and is positioned as a health-conscious fast-casual option in Singapore food courts and cafes.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo database (cr_id F05100147) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and cholesterol adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite poke bowl data and documented as adapted. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database sushi rice reference, which is the dominant carbohydrate component. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.9 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (1.8 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.6 g/100 g), potassium_mg (220 mg/100 g), and cholesterol_mg (18 mg/100 g) are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite poke bowl data (closest approved-source equivalent) as HPB Foodo does not report these sub-fractions separately. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite tuna poké bowl preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a matched composite entry as of 2026-04-16. 3. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 (confirmed zero): raw tuna and plant-based toppings contain no industrial trans fats; confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central component data. 4. The input record incorrectly listed dietary_tags as ['vegetarian', 'plant_based', 'low_calorie']; these have been corrected — the dish contains raw fish and is neither vegetarian nor plant-based. 5. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on sushi rice as the dominant carbohydrate source; no direct composite poke bowl GI value exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 6. Tuna consumption should be limited to 2–3 servings per week due to mercury content, per Singapore HPB and international food safety guidance. 7. FoodEx2 code A0FJP used as closest defensible analogue for a composite rice dish with fish; no dedicated poke bowl code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2026.
